coredata.dc:title: Resonance energy transfer between ZnCdHgSe quantum dots and gold nanorods enhancing photoelectrochemical immunosensing of prostate specific antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanying
Yu, Xiangyang
Ye, Xiaoxue
Wu, Kangbing
Wu, Tsunghsueh
Li, Chunya
coredata.dc:description: Gold nanorods (AuNRs) integrated with ZnCdHgSe near-infrared quantum dots (AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs) were successfully synthesized and characterized by transmission electron microscope, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction. A glassy carbon electrode was decorated with the aforementioned AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs nanocomposite, which provides a biocompatible interface for the subsequent immobilization of prostate specific antibody (anti-PSA). After being successively treated with glutaraldehyde vapor and bovine serum albumin solution, a photoelectrochemical immunosensing platform based on anti-PSA/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE was established. The photocurrent response of ZnCdHgSe QDs was tremendously improved by AuNRs due to the effect of resonance energy transfer which can be deduced from the dependence of the enhanced efficiency on the AuNRs with different length-to-diameter ratios and spectral absorption characteristics. A maximum photocurrent was obtained when the absorption spectrum of AuNRs matched well with the emission spectrum of ZnCdHgSe QDs. A photoelectrochemical immunosensor for prostate specific antigen (PSA) was achieved by monitoring the photocurrent variation. The photocurrent variation before and after being interacted with PSA solution exhibits a good linear relationship with the logarithm of its concentration (logc PSA) in the range from 1.0 pg mL−1 to 50.0 ng mL−1. The detection limit of this photoelectrochemical immunosensor is able to reach 0.1 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Determining PSA in clinical human serum was also demonstrated by using the developed anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE electrode. The results were comparable with those obtained from an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical immunosensing
Resonance energy transfer
ZnCdHgSe QDs
Gold nanorods
Prostate specific antigen

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A), UV–vis absorption spectra (B) and enhanced photocurrent responses (D) of gold nanorods produced by controlling the reaction time at 15 h (a, a′, a″), 20 h (b, b′, b″) and 24 h (c, c′, c″); C is the emission spectrum of ZnCdHgSe QDs (d) and UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNRs (e); f is the photoelectrochemical response of ZnCdHgSe QDs.

Nyquist plots (A) and typical photoelectrochemical response (B) of GCE (a), AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (b, b′), anti-PSA/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (c, c′), anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (d, d′), PSA/anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (e, e′).
SEM images of a bare GCE (a), an AuNRs/GCE (b), an AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (c) and an anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (d).

Typical photocurrent response (A) and calibration curve (B) for PSA determination at the anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE. (Error bars are standard deviation for three independent determinations).

Typical photocurrent responses of the anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE towards PSA in the real serum samples. (Curve a, b, c and d is corresponding to sample 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, listed in Table 2).
Comparing the Sensing Performances of the Immunosensors for PSA assay.

row.entry: ITO electrode modified with gold and palladium, and flower-like titanium dioxide microparticles
ZnO nanorods modified reduced graphene oxide-paper electrode and silver deposition-induced signal amplification strategy
Flexible Gold Electrode Array for Multiplexed

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of clinical serum samples using the developed photoelectrochemical immunosensor and ELISA method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Resonance energy transfer between ZnCdHgSe quantum dots and gold nanorods enhancing photoelectrochemical immunosensing of prostate specific antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry of the State Ethnic Affairs Commission, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry of the State Ethnic Affairs Commission
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Wisconsin-Platteville, 1 University Plaza, Platteville, WI 53818-3099, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold nanorods (AuNRs) integrated with ZnCdHgSe near-infrared quantum dots (AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs) were successfully synthesized and characterized by transmission electron microscope, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction. A glassy carbon electrode was decorated with the aforementioned AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs nanocomposite, which provides a biocompatible interface for the subsequent immobilization of prostate specific antibody (anti-PSA). After being successively treated with glutaraldehyde vapor and bovine serum albumin solution, a photoelectrochemical immunosensing platform based on anti-PSA/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE was established. The photocurrent response of ZnCdHgSe QDs was tremendously improved by AuNRs due to the effect of resonance energy transfer which can be deduced from the dependence of the enhanced efficiency on the AuNRs with different length-to-diameter ratios and spectral absorption characteristics. A maximum photocurrent was obtained when the absorption spectrum of AuNRs matched well with the emission spectrum of ZnCdHgSe QDs. A photoelectrochemical immunosensor for prostate specific antigen (PSA) was achieved by monitoring the photocurrent variation. The photocurrent variation before and after being interacted with PSA solution exhibits a good linear relationship with the logarithm of its concentration (logc PSA) in the range from 1.0 pg mL−1 to 50.0 ng mL−1. The detection limit of this photoelectrochemical immunosensor is able to reach 0.1 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Determining PSA in clinical human serum was also demonstrated by using the developed anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE electrode. The results were comparable with those obtained from an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay method.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical technique has become a newly developed approach for the fabrication of biosensors [1,2]. The merits of the photoelectrochemical sensing system, such as simple apparatus, low cost, and facile fabrication, are inherited from the superior properties of electrochemical and photochemical sensing techniques [3]. Due to the complete separation of the excitation source from the detection signal, the background signal can be subtracted, thus leading to high sensitivity and excellent sensing performance [4–6]. Thus, photoelectrochemical approaches are most pertinent to become a sensing platform for biomacromolecules at ultralow concentration, especially for DNA, RNA, and proteins [7–10]. In this technique, the photocurrent generated by the specific recognition, steric-hindrance effect, enzymatic reaction, and energy transfer is used as the detection signal for quantitative analysis of biomolecules [11].

Energy transfer has been demonstrated to be a suitable candidate for the sensitive determination of biomolecules by improving the charge dissociation and transport efficiency [12–14]. In a photoelectrochemical sensing system, energy transfer typically involves a non-radiative transfer process in which the excitation energy is transferred from an excited donor to a proximal acceptor [15]. Energy transfer is a consequence of a long-range dipole-dipole interaction between the donors and the acceptors. It is extremely dependent on the donor-acceptor distance and their relative dipole orientations. The overlap of the spectral band between the donor and the acceptor (>30%) and the mutual distance (<10 nm) are crucial factors for effective energy transfer [16]. Semiconductors are commonly used as donors to produce excitation resource. Among them, quantum dots are considered to be the prominent candidates for photoactive materials due to their fascinating characteristics such as excellent photostability, broad excitation spectral range, adjustable emission wavelength, and superior biocompatibility. Unfortunately, some of the quantum dots (ZnS, CdS etc.) possess large band gap and thus require high-energy excitation light sources to produce photocurrent [17]. They often encounter many difficulties such as photocorrosion under illumination, deactivating biomolecules and low photoelectrochemical conversation efficiency, thus leading to instability, poor sensitivity, and selectivity for biosensing.

In order to overcome these challenges, some semiconductors possessing narrow band gap were successfully developed [18–20]. They can be excited by a long wavelength light source, preventing damage to biomaterials and improving the photoelectrochemical sensing performance towards target molecules. Among them, near-infrared (NIR) emitting QDs have been considered as the suitable candidates for biosensing, bioimaging, and photoelectrochemical determination. Recently, quaternary ZnCdHgSe QDs are successfully synthesized in our group and have been demonstrated their capability of being effectively excited at 690 nm [21]. Although sensitive photoelectrochemical response can be obtained by layer-by-layer assembling ZnCdHgSe QDs and polymerized ionic liquid film, an effective method for enhancing the photocurrent response under visible or near IR illumination is desirable for fabricating a biosensing system based on ZnCdHgSe QDs. Energy transfer can be an excellent approach to enhance the photoelectric conversion efficiency by improving the electron-hole pair separation efficiency. Noble metal nanomaterials, especially gold, are well-known to be good energy acceptors due to their unique features, such as surface plasmon resonance, high extinction coefficient and broad absorption spectra [22–24]. Therefore, gold nanorods (AuNRs) were employed as acceptors to enhance the photocurrent response of ZnCdHgSe QDs generated from the effects of energy transfer and high conductivity. In an energy-transfer based photo- or photoelectrochemical sensing system, the spectral matching of the donor and the acceptor is a crucial element to influence the intensity of photocurrent. Thus, gold nanorods with different morphological characteristics were fabricated to investigate the effects of the absorbance properties of gold nanorods on the photocurrent response of ZnCdHgSe QDs.

To evaluate the photoelectrochemical sensing performance of AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs nanocomposites, anti-human PSA monoclonal antibody (anti-PSA) was selected as a model protein to develop a biosensing system. Gold nanorods not only supplied some active sites to anchor anti-PSA onto the photoelectrochemical platform, but also exhibited some excellent properties to improve the photocurrent response. As the maximal absorption wavelength of gold nanorods is matched well with the maximal emission wavelength of ZnCdHgSe QDs, the maximum photocurrent response can be produced. When the specific immunoreaction between the anti-PSA and PSA was performed, the blocking effect of PSA on the mass- and electron transfer of ascorbic acid will cause the photocurrent response decreasing. The variation of photocurrent response was used for the quantitative assay of PSA concentration. All stepwise processes for the AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs preparation and the PSA immunosensor fabrication were thoroughly characterized with transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical techniques. The photoelectrochemical sensing performances of the AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs based biosensor towards PSA were evaluated. The practical application of the as-prepared biosensor was also demonstrated by determining PSA concentration in real clinical serum samples.

N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC) was bought from Aladdin Reagent Inc. (Shanghai, China). Cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), bovine serum albumin (BSA), selenium powder, NaBH4, HAuCl4, ZnCl2, glutaraldehyde and ascorbic acid (AA) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). CdCl2·2.5H2O were bought from Chengdu Chemical Reagent Plant (Chengdu, China). HgCl2 was supplied by Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Mouse anti-human PSA monoclonal antibody (anti-PSA), prostate specific antigen (PSA), α–fetoprotein, and human IgG were purchased from Beijing Biosynthesis Biotechnology Co., LTD (Beijing, China). Other reagents were analytical grade and were used as received. Clinical serum samples were kindly supplied by the central hospital of Wuhan (Wuhan, China).

Transmission electron microscopic characterization of AuNRs was performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 20S-TWIN instrument (FEI Company, Netherlands). Interfacial morphology of all biosensors was characterized using a scanning electron microscopy (Sirion 200 microscope, FEI). The fluorescence spectrum of ZnCdHgSe QDs was obtained on a LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). UV–vis analysis of AuNRs was carried out on a Lambda Bio 35 (PerkinElmer, USA). AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensors can be illuminated with a 5 W output white light LED lamp to produce photocurrent response. Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical experiments were carried out on a CHI 920D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Corp., Shanghai, China). An electrochemical system including a glassy carbon electrode (Ø = 3 mm) as the substrate, a Pt wire counter-electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was employed to characterize the photoelectrochemical sensing performance. Photocurrent responses of all sensors were measured in a phosphate buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) using 0.2 mol L−1 ascorbic acid as an electron donor. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was obtained in a KCl solution (0.1 mol L−1) containing 5.0 mmol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. The frequency was controlled over the range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz. PSA concentration in human serum samples was determined using an AU5421 automatic biochemical analyzer (Olympus Optical Co., Ltd., Japan). The accuracy of the AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensor was confirmed by ELISA that was achieved using a commercial kit (Leadman Group Co., Ltd., China) and was successfully conducted based on the manufacturer's guidance.

ZnCdHgSe QDs were prepared according to the previously reported procedure [21]. Being kept in an ice-bath, selenium powder was added into a NaBH4 solution to provide a NaHSe solution. ZnCl2 solution (15 mL, 5 mmol L−1), CdCl2 solution (1 mL, 5 mmol L−1), HgCl2 solution (5 mL, 5 mmol L−1), and NAC solution (5 mL, 50 mmol L−1) were successively added into a flask and were thoroughly mixed. The solution deaerated with nitrogen was adjusted to a pH value of 11.10 using NaOH solution (1.0 mol L−1). Subsequently, the mixture was diluted with ultrapure water to 50 mL. Thereafter, the aforementioned NaHSe solution was injected into the sealed flask as quickly as possible. After being kept at 80 °C for 4 h with vigorous stirring, the reaction system was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, ZnCdHgSe QDs were transferred into a Millipore ultrafiltration centrifuge tube, and were separated at a centrifugation speed of 10000 rpm for 15 min to purify.

A seed-mediated method was employed for gold nanorods preparation [25]. First, freshly prepared NaBH4 solution (0.12 mL, 0.01 mol L−1) was quickly injected into a mixture solution of HAuCl4 (1 mL, 0.5 mmol L−1) and CTAB (1 mL, 0.2 mol L−1) to yield a solution containing gold seeds. Then, gold seed solution (40 μL), HAuCl4 solution (6 mL, 5 mmol L−1), CTAB solution (30 mL, 0.25 mol L−1), AgNO3 solution (75 μL, 0.1 mol L−1), HCl solution (67 μL, 1.2 mol L−1) were loaded into a flask. Ascorbic acid (3.5 mL, 0.01 mol L−1) was introduced into the mixture solution as a reducing agent to produce gold nanorods. After being stirred for 10 min, the solution was allowed to stand for 15 h, 20 h, and 24 h, respectively. A centrifugation procedure was performed sequentially to separate gold nanorods.

1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (10 mg mL−1), N-hydroxysuccinimide (20 mg mL−1), and ZnCdHgSe QDs (4 mL) were added to a readily mixed solution of mercaptoacetic acid (0.01 mol L−1, 4 mL) and gold nanorods (15 mg m L−1, 1 mL). After being kept at room temperature for 4 h, the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe nanocomposites were purified by centrifugation and thorough washing with ultrapure water. The as-prepared AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe nanocomposites were characterized with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction. The data shown in Fig. S 1 and Fig. S 2 confirmed the successful integration of ZnCdHgSe QDs onto AuNRs surface.

After being polished with alumina slurries, an L-like glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was ultrasonicated in a nitric acid solution (1:1), ethanol, and water to give a cleared surface. Then, the GCE was modified with AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs at the volume of 5.5 μL, and was stored at room temperature to evaporate all solvents. After that, anti-PSA solution (5.5 μL, 10 μg mL−1) was loaded onto the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE electrode surface, and was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator overnight. After being gently washed using phosphate buffer, the sensing interface was inserted into a vial filled with glutaraldehyde vapor for 5 min. Hereafter, bovine serum albumin solution (0.25%) was used to block all residual aldehyde groups, resulting a photoelectrochemical immunosensor based on the structure of anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE. In photoelectrochemical measurements, PSA solution (5.5 μL) was coated onto the immunosensor surface, and an incubation process was fulfilled under the temperature of 35 °C for 20 min. A schematic for the photoelectrochemical immunosensor fabrication and the photoelectrochemical response mechanism was illustrated in Scheme S 1.

Synthesized gold nanorods and ZnCdHgSe QDs were characterized by various techniques. Fig. 1 (A) presents the transmission electron microscopic images of gold nanorods prepared by controlling the reaction time at 15 h (a), 20 h (b) and 24 h (c). It was obvious to see that the morphology of the as-synthesized gold nanorods was rod-like nanoparticles. The length-to-diameter ratio of gold nanorods was increased with the reaction time prolonging. UV–Vis absorption spectrum of these gold nanorods shown in Fig. 1 (B) determined the maximum absorption wavelengths of these gold nanorods were 718 nm (a′), 778 nm (b′), and 868 nm (c′), respectively. The results indicated that the optical properties of gold nanorods can be tuned by controlling their morphological characteristics. To obtain high efficiency in energy transfer, it is very important to ensure the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of ZnCdHgSe QDs and the absorption spectrum of gold nanorods. From Fig. 1 (C), ZnCdHgSe QDs exhibited a fluorescence emission peak at 688 nm (curve d), which suggests to work best with the gold nanorods produced from the reaction time of 15 h with an absorption peak at about 718 nm (curve e). Apparently, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CdZnHgSe QDs overlaps a great deal within the absorption spectrum of gold nanorods, enabling efficient traits for the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between them. Upon integration CdZnHgSe QDs with gold nanorods as photoelectrochemical sensing platform (Fig. 1 D), no matter what type of gold nanorods was used (a″, b″, c″), the photocurrent responses are dramatically higher than that obtained just using CdZnHgSe QDs (f) alone as photoactive materials. Notably, when gold nanorods, produced by controlling reaction time of 15 h, were employed to fabricate a photoelectrochemical sensing interface, a maximum photocurrent was obtained, leading to a significant increase in sensitivity for biosensing applications. In the further investigations, such gold nanorods were confirmed as a suitable choice for the photoelectrochemical immunosensor fabrication.

To confirm the successful integration gold nanorods with ZnCdHgSe QDs, TEM and SEM were utilized to characterize the as-synthesized nanocomposites, respectively. As shown in Fig. S 3, after covalent modification of ZnCdHgSe QDs onto the gold nanorods surface, a significant change can be observed in the morphology of AuNRs. AuNRs seem to be embedded in an organic component. Although ZnCdHgSe QDs were too small to be observed in SEM, their presence in the nanocomposites was confirmed by the observed the current responses of these photoelectrochemical sensing interfaces (Fig. S 4).

The development process of the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensing interface was characterized using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical technique. Nyquist plots of these electrochemical interfaces obtained from the sequential steps during the immunosensor fabrication were presented in Fig. 2 (A). The equivalent electrical circuit as shown in the inset was used to calculate electrochemical parameters from these Nyquist plots. Herein, R s, R ct, Z w, and C dl represent the resistance of electrolyte solution, charge transfer resistance, Warburg impedance and the double-layer capacitance, respectively [26–28]. Clearly, the diameter of the semicircle, which means the charge transfer resistance, increased significantly with AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs, anti-PSA, BSA, and PSA being successively assembled on the GCE surface subsequently. The corresponding R ct values of GCE (a), AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (b), anti-PSA/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (c), anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (d), PSA/anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (e) were 255.5 Ω, 1632.0 Ω, 2175 Ω, 3482 Ω and 6084 Ω, respectively. The poor conductivity of proteins led to such an increase in the charge transfer resistance due to their blocking effect on the electron transfer and the mass transport of K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 probes. The photoelectrochemical response of the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensing system in an ascorbic acid solution was a valuable mean to probe the fabrication process. It was observed from Fig. 2 (B) that the photoelectrochemical response of AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (b′) decreased significantly with successive immobilizing of anti-PSA (c′) and BSA (d′). Especially when PSA was selectively recognized by anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (e′), a dramatic decrease can be observed on the photocurrent response, demonstrating high sensitivity for PSA determination. The decline in photocurrent may come from the blocking effect of these proteins towards the diffusion of ascorbic acid, thus prohibiting the consuming photogenerated holes. As a result, the chance for the electron-hole recombination increased. Accordingly, the generated electron-hole pairs will be difficult to be separated to produce the photocurrent, leading to the signal intensity decrease.

The morphology of a GCE (a), AuNRs/GCE (b), AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (c) and anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE (d) surface was characterized with scanning electron microscopy, and was shown in Fig. 3 . Glassy carbon electrode originally had a smooth surface. After being modified by AuNRs, some rod-shape nanoparticles were observed clearly and well distributed on the entire electrode surface. As AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs and anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs were successively modified onto the GCE surface, a distinct morphology was exhibited with gold nanorods being embedded into a network structure. Notably, after anti-PSA being immobilized, gold nanorods were nearly hard to see. These observations have demonstrated that the fabrication process has been progressed successfully.

Experimental conditions for PSA determination based on the aforementioned photoelectrochemical immunosensor were optimized using the photocurrent response as an evaluation standard. Before photoelectrochemical measurements, the immunosensor was incubated in a PSA solution for the specific recognition. The incubation time and temperature were determined as critical parameters that will significantly affected the sensitivity of the photoelectrochemical immunosensor towards PSA. The influence of the incubation time on the photocurrent response of the immunosensor towards 5.0 ng mL−1 PSA was presented in Fig. S 5. As the incubation time varied from 5 min to 20 min, a gradual increase can be observed towards the photocurrent variation (ΔI), the difference in the photocurrent response of the anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE before and after the immunoreaction with PSA, was observed. With a further prolonging the incubation time in the range of 20–40 min, the photocurrent variation can be neglected, meaning almost all active sites have been occupied and an equilibrium state has been achieved for the recognition of PSA through an immunoreaction. Accordingly, the incubation process was accomplished in a PSA solution for 20 min.

The temperature will affect the activity and diffusion speed of biomolecules, and further influence the performance of the as-prepared immunosensor. Thus, the influence of the incubation temperature on the photocurrent variation (ΔI) of the immunosensor towards 5.0 ng mL−1 PSA was investigated and exhibited in Fig. S 6. With incubation temperature increasing from 20 °C to 35 °C, an obvious enhancement can be observed on the photocurrent variation and a maximum value can be obtained at 35 °C. Beyond 35 °C, further increasing the incubation temperature caused the photocurrent variation to decline dramatically. The deactivation of biomacromolecules due to the high temperature is the main reason for such decline. Therefore, a temperature of 35 °C was selected as the suitable incubation temperature for the specific recognition of PSA by the photoelectrochemical immunosensor.

Fig. 4 (A) shows the typical photoelectrochemical response curves for the anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE towards PSA at different concentrations (c PSA). It was found that the photocurrent intensity is inversely proportional to PSA concentration, giving a decrease in photocurrent response with an increase in PSA concentration. Taking the consistent trend in photocurrent response corresponding to the PSA concentration, the fabricated immunosensing system can be further developed into PSA assay. As shown in Fig. 4 (B), there is a good linear relationship between the percentage of the photocurrent decline [ΔI/I 0 = (I 0 − I)/I 0] and the logarithm of PSA concentration (logc PSA) in the range of 1.0 pg mL−1 ∼ 50.0 ng mL−1, where I 0 and I are the photocurrent response of the anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE before and after being performed the immune recognition process in PSA solution. The linear equation can be described as following: ΔI/I 0 = 0.1102 logc PSA (ng mL−1) + 0.4335 (R2 = 0.997). The detection limit is calculated to be 0.10 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). The analytical characteristics of this photoelectrochemical immunosensor were compared with the previously reported methods. It can be seen from Table 1 that the aforementioned photoelectrochemical immunosensor exhibits some superior characteristics for PSA determination.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed approach, the photocurrent responses of the anti-PSA(BSA)/AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE towards 0.1 ng mL−1 of PSA in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 5.0 ng mL−1 of α-fetoprotein, neuron specific enolase (c) and human IgG (d) were investigated independently and displayed in Fig. S 7. The variation of photocurrent intensity can be negligible, meaning that nonspecific absorption has no obvious influence on the immune recognition reaction between the anti-PSA and PSA. The results also demonstrated that the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs based photoelectrochemical immunosensing system possesses excellent selectivity, and will be suitable for PSA determination in real samples with a complicated matrix.

The reproducibility for the photoelectrochemical immunosensor was examined by determining 5.0 ng mL−1 of PSA with four immunosensors which were prepared independently. A standard deviation of 4.96% suggested that the established immunosensor fabrication process is repeatable and well-controlled. The stability is another essential characteristic for an immunosensor, and thus was checked in detail in this study. After being stored in a phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at 4 °C for four weeks, the photocurrent response decline for 5.0 ng mL−1 of PSA was 5.8%, demonstrating a photoelectrochemical immunosensor has been designed with a good stability. The photoelectrochemical response of the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE under photoexcitation process was also investigated by controlling the time interval at the scale of 10 s. The photocurrent response of an AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE were recorded and illustrated in Fig. S 8. The photocurrent intensity shows only a negligible variation, even not be perceived. The native photoelectrochemical quality of the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs/GCE supplies an advantageous guarantee for the immunosensor to maintain its high stable response to PSA.

To validate the practicability of the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensor, PSA in clinical human serum was determined using the aforementioned method. After being diluted with phosphate buffer at the ratio of 1: 100, the PSA concentrations in the clinical serum samples were directly analyzed using the as-fabricated immunosensor. Typical photocurrent responses of the aforementioned immunosensor towards PSA in real clinical samples were presented in Fig. 5 . To verify the reliability of the immunosensor, the clinical serum samples were also assayed by ELISA method. All calculated values were listed in Table 2 . The deviations for the PSA concentrations measured independently using the immunosensor and ELISA method are less than 5%, indicating that the AuNRs-ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensor is highly comparable to commonly accepted ELISA for PSA determination in real samples.

A novel photoelectrochemical immunosensor for PSA was fabricated by using AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs nanocomposites as photoelectric conversion materials and anti-PSA as the receptor. It is interesting to note that the morphological characteristics of AuNRs exhibit a significant influence on the photocurrent response of ZnCdHgSe QDs. AuNRs not only supplied some active sites for anti-PSA immobilizing, but also played an important role as an acceptor in improving the electron-hole pair separation efficiency of ZnCdHgSe QDs, and thus enhancing the photoelectrochemical performances of the as-fabricated immunosensor towards PSA. When the absorbance spectrum of AuNRs matches well with the emission spectrum of ZnCdHgSe QDs, a maximum photocurrent response was obtained, enabling the quantitative determination of PSA. The results suggest that the AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs based immunosensing system possesses high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility, and most important ability to determine PSA concentrations in real clinical serum samples. Owing to the excellent photoelectrochemical characteristics, the established AuNRs/ZnCdHgSe QDs interface proves to be a promising biosensing platform for the construction of diverse biosensors just by substituting the bio-recognition elements such as antibodies, cells, and organisms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from The National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275166 and 21675175) and The Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No.2015CFA092).
The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.09.015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical biosensors: new insights into promising photoelectrodes and signal amplification strategies
Photoelectrochemical bioanalysis: the state of the art
